justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Jurisdiction-specific rules identified for all 3 jurisdictions — California §226/AB5 requirements in BR-3/BR-6/BR-8, Germany works council in BR-4, Indonesia contractor classification in BR-6/AC-4. (2) Germany works council approval identified as a blocking pre-condition via BR-4 with feature flag gating and AC-2 showing feature unavailability without approval. (3) Driver classification data model difference explicitly called out in BR-6 (employee in CA vs. independent_contractor in Indonesia) with downstream impact on disclosure fields. (4) Formal escalations for legal review present — three structured escalations requiring legal counsel confirmation before implementation. (5) No single global acceptance criteria set — AC-1 through AC-4 are jurisdiction-specific with different field requirements per market. (6) confidence.level = 52, well below the 60 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough jurisdiction-specific analysis — each jurisdiction gets distinct business rules, acceptance criteria, and risk assessment rather than a one-size-fits-all approach
Germany works council requirement treated as a hard gate (BR-4 with feature flag disabled until approval record exists), not just a mention — includes revocation edge case
Driver classification correctly identified as a structural invariant (BR-6) that gates downstream compensation logic, with explicit CA=employee, ID=contractor mapping
Confidence level of 52 is appropriately low, with well-articulated low-confidence areas including cross-border jurisdiction resolution and Indonesian contractor disclosure requirements
Business impact section explicitly states Germany cannot launch on same timeline as other markets — phased rollout requirement surfaced
Caught the subtle trap about real-time display potentially constituting a legal wage statement under §226, which would change field requirements — escalated appropriately
Edge cases are strong — works council revocation, cross-border deliveries, mid-period minimum wage changes all identified
Escalations are structured with options and recommendations rather than just flagging problems — gives stakeholders actionable paths forward

Did not explicitly surface the trap that removing the transparency feature in California (e.g., to simplify codebase) would be illegal while removing it in Indonesia would be fine — the asymmetric removal risk is implied but not called out directly

Rejection reasons array is empty — Bird could have more forcefully rejected producing implementation-ready criteria by stating that legal review is a prerequisite, rather than providing detailed ACs with a caveat

grader_results.detail: field escalations[*].type equals check failed: expected 'ambiguity', got values: 'missing_context', 'missing_context'
